coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of boron-doped carbon dots from Chinese herbal residues for Fe3+ sensing, anti-counterfeiting, and photodegradation applications
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lianglin
Luo, Weikang
Chen, Yao
Zheng, Jun
Cao, Lina
Duan, Lihua
Tang, Tao
Wang, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), especially from biomass doped with heteroatoms, have drawn increasing attention owing to their versatility in potential applications. In this work, boron-doped CDs derived from Chinese herbal residues (B@HRCDs) were fabricated through a simple, green, one-step solvothermal method, resulting in B@HRCDs with a uniform size of 2.8 nm. The synthesized B@HRCDs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, UV–visible spectroscopy, Transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and Fluorescence techniques. As prepared, the B@HRCDs demonstrated a bright fluorescence with a quantum yield of 4.0% and displayed exceptional selectivity towards Fe3+. In vitro experiments, they did not exhibit cytotoxicity against BV2 cells. The synthesized B@HRCDs were used as a sensitive nanoprobe for the detection of free Fe3+ in the range of 0–80 μM with a minimum limit of detection (LOD) of 1.08 μM. Based on their biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity, B@HRCDs were also shown to be useful for intracellular Fe3+ detection applications. When fluorescent ink was applied to bond paper, the patterns were clearly visible under UV light, indicating that B@HRCDs can be used for message encryption. Finally, it was further shown that the degradation rate of indigo carmine after 5 h of sunlight irradiation was 91% in the presence of B@HRCDs and decreased to 61% after 5 cycles, which indicates that the B@HRCDs have favorable photocatalytic activity and photostability. It is thus believed that this strategy achieves the reuse of herbal residues and proposes a low-cost, green method for the synthesis of CDs with Fe3+ sensing, anti-counterfeiting, and photodegradation functions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the B@HRCDs (HRTEM image in inset) (a). The size distribution of B@HRCDs was estimated by counting 50 particles (b). XRD pattern of B@HRCDs powders (c). FT-IR spectrum of B@HRCDs in the 600–3800 spectral region (d).
XPS survey spectra of B@HRCDs (a) and high-resolution XPS patterns of C1s (b), N1s (c), and B1s (d).
UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of B@HRCDs (a). Emission intensity under various excitation wavelengths (b).

Fluorescence emission intensity of B@HRCDs upon adding different concentrations of Fe3+ (a). The dependence of F/F0 on the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0–320 μM (inset: the linear relationship of F/F0 versus the concentration of Fe3+ over the range of 0–80 μM), where F and F0 represent emission intensity in the presence and absence of Fe3+, respectively (b). Fluorescence emission intensity ratio F/F0 of B@HRCDs following the addition of various metal ions at a concentration of 200 μM (c). Changes of emission intensity after adding EDTA chelator (F/F0), Fe3+, and a mixture of Fe3+ and EDTA (d). The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements.
Schematic illustration of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of B@HRCDs in the presence of Fe3+.

Measurement of BV2 cell viability after incubation with B@HRCDs for 24 h (a). Bright field images of HeLa cells (b and d) and fluorescence images with excitation at 480 nm (c and e). Cells were incubated with 400 μg/mL B@HRCDs at 37 °C for 12 h (b and c). First, the cells were incubated with culture medium containing B@HRCDs for 12 h, followed by incubation with 400 μM FeCl3·6H2O at 37 °C for 6 h (d and e). The scale bar is 100 μm. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements.

Photograph of indigo carmine solution in the presence of B@HRCDs with/without sunlight irradiation (a). Hydroxyl radical-mediated degradation process of indigo carmine (b). Absorption intensity of indigo carmine at different intervals in sunlight (c). Schematic diagram of hydroxyl radical generation of B@HRCDs (d). The indigo carmine photodegradation efficiencies with the addition of trapping agents BQ and IPA after exposure to sunlight (e). Recycling tests of indigo carmine photodegradation efficiencies (f).

Anti-counterfeiting of B@HRCDs. The letters “CARBON DOTS” under sunlight and 254 nm (a). Patterns showing a dog, an elephant and a dolphin under sunlight and 254 nm (b). Hand-cut paper in different shapes stained with B@HRCDs in sunlight and under UV light at 385 nm (c).
Schematic diagram of the process of green one-step solvothermal treatment for the synthesis of B@HRCDs using herbal residues and an application to Fe3+ sensing.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of boron-doped carbon dots from Chinese herbal residues for Fe3+ sensing, anti-counterfeiting, and photodegradation applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), especially from biomass doped with heteroatoms, have drawn increasing attention owing to their versatility in potential applications. In this work, boron-doped CDs derived from Chinese herbal residues (B@HRCDs) were fabricated through a simple, green, one-step solvothermal method, resulting in B@HRCDs with a uniform size of 2.8 nm. The synthesized B@HRCDs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, UV–visible spectroscopy, Transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and Fluorescence techniques. As prepared, the B@HRCDs demonstrated a bright fluorescence with a quantum yield of 4.0% and displayed exceptional selectivity towards Fe3+. In vitro experiments, they did not exhibit cytotoxicity against BV2 cells. The synthesized B@HRCDs were used as a sensitive nanoprobe for the detection of free Fe3+ in the range of 0–80 μM with a minimum limit of detection (LOD) of 1.08 μM. Based on their biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity, B@HRCDs were also shown to be useful for intracellular Fe3+ detection applications. When fluorescent ink was applied to bond paper, the patterns were clearly visible under UV light, indicating that B@HRCDs can be used for message encryption. Finally, it was further shown that the degradation rate of indigo carmine after 5 h of sunlight irradiation was 91% in the presence of B@HRCDs and decreased to 61% after 5 cycles, which indicates that the B@HRCDs have favorable photocatalytic activity and photostability. It is thus believed that this strategy achieves the reuse of herbal residues and proposes a low-cost, green method for the synthesis of CDs with Fe3+ sensing, anti-counterfeiting, and photodegradation functions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Chinese herbal medicine has been used in China for thousands of years, and it refers to medicines that are used to prevent or treat diseases under the guidance of Chinese medical theory and clinical experience. Most Chinese herbal medicines come from the roots, stems, and leaves of plants, and they have a large market in China. Interestingly, many herbs originated from foods, such as Rhizoma glycyrrhizae, Semen prunus, Radix platycodonis, and Radix Angelicae sinensis, have both nutritional and medicinal value, and have been used as edible and medicinal plants for a long time in many countries, including China, Korea, and Japan (Jiang et al., 2020). However, in the process of its industrialization, millions of tons of Chinese medicine residues are produced every year (Lu and Li, 2021). They are usually treated as waste after decoction and are discarded by incineration, in landfills, and in piles in fixed areas, creating very serious environmental pollution and wasting resources (Zhang et al., 2022c). Thus, treatment of biowaste with reasonable methods is particularly important. Various methods have been implemented to utilize these wastes, including converting them into bioethanol, biofuel, and carbon-based materials in the industry (Anker et al., 2016).

It is worth mentioning that Chinese herbal biomass has become an important source of carbon materials due to the presence of large amounts of cellulose, lignin, and polysaccharides in waste herbal residues (Huang et al., 2021). In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have gained significant attention among researchers due to their unique properties, such as small particle size, high fluorescence stability (Huo et al., 2022), easy surface functionalization (Li et al., 2020), and excellent biocompatibility (Liu et al., 2020a). These attributes have propelled CDs to the forefront of various fields, including bioimaging (Chung et al., 2021), drug delivery (Hou et al., 2021), and optoelectronic devices (Zhao et al., 2021), offering promising application prospects. CDs can be used to construct novel fluorescent probes for the determination of important metal or nonmetal ions, small molecules (Chao et al., 2020), and macromolecules (Luo et al., 2021). According to the literature, various green sources have been used for the synthesis of CDs, including rice husk (Wang et al., 2016), honey (Chowdhury et al., 2020), banana peels (Nguyen et al., 2022). Numerous CDs derived from biomass have been used to detect metal ions through fluorescence quenching generated by ligand-mediated electron transfer between functional groups and metal ions (Wang et al., 2020).

Iron is vital for all living systems and widely distributed in the environment. It plays a crucial role in physiological processes like cellular metabolism, oxygen transport, and enzymatic reactions (van Swelm et al., 2020). However, imbalances in iron levels can lead to health issues, including heart disease, cancer, and organ dysfunctions (Picard et al., 2020). Various analytical methods, such as spectrophotometry, voltammetry, and fluorescence sensing, are used for metal ion detection (Zhang et al., 2019). Among these, fluorescence sensing offers advantages such as convenience, fast response, selectivity, and high sensitivity in detecting trace metal ion concentrations (Ankireddy and Kim, 2018). In recent years, an increasing amount of research has focused on utilizing CDs for Fe3+ detection (Zhang et al., 2022a). The primary challenge associated with CDs as fluorescent probes is the requirement for both high quantum yields and selectivity. It has been established that CDs allow for doping and surface modification to improve their quantum yields and ion selectivity (Zhang et al., 2022b). A significant advantage of utilizing biomass as a carbon source for CDs is its remarkable quantum yield, facilitating self-doping (especially N-doping) without requiring additional reagents or modifications (Liu et al., 2020b). Recently, a research study was conducted to synthesize CDs using Kentucky bluegrass, achieving exceptional detection limits of Fe3+ as low as 1.4 μM and quantum yields of up to 7.0% (Krishnaiah et al., 2022). However, B-doped biomass-derived CDs are fewer reported.

Fraudulent practices in the field of drugs and food items can seriously endanger human health. Some security technologies, including simple markers, holograms, plasma security labels, and security inks, have been developed over the past few decades as a shield against counterfeiting (Abdollahi et al., 2020). CDs as anti-counterfeiting inks have attracted the attention of researchers owing to their advantages of good fluorescence stability, low cost, simple fabrication, and eco-friendliness. Mopan persimmons served as a carbon source for synthesizing CDs and were effectively utilized for anti-counterfeiting purposes by combining them with sugar (Ma et al., 2023). Thus, biomass derived CDs have great potential for anti-counterfeiting applications.

Organic dyes, as pollutants, cause serious environmental and health problems owing to the difficulty of spontaneous degradation (He et al., 2018). CDs as photoluminescent materials with the ability to produce reactive oxygen species, can be used as catalysts for the degradation of pollutants (Chen et al., 2017). However, the lower visible light utilization results in the inefficient photocatalysis of CDs. Biomass-derived CDs are rich in functional groups that can modulate the ability to generate reactive oxygen species and thus display high catalytic activity.

In this work, Chinese herbal residues were used as raw materials to construct CDs by B-doping, which has good selectivity for Fe3+. Given the good biocompatibility of B@HRCDs, the presence of Fe3+ in the HeLa cell environment was detected. B@HRCDs were also used as anti-counterfeiting ink, and the information on the bond paper was clearly visible under UV light. Additionally, the B@HRCDs exhibited superior photocatalytic activity in sunlight. The degradation efficiency of indigo carmine solution was 91% by 5 h of irradiation with sunlight in the presence of B@HRCDs and remained at 61% after 5 cycles. This work proposes an effective strategy to optimize resource utilization by converting herbal residues waste into smart nanomaterials through a solvothermal reaction.

B@HRCDs were synthesized via a solvothermal method, and their structure was characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), and zeta potential analyses. The optical properties of the B@HRCDs were assessed through fluorescence spectra. Furthermore, the toxicity of the B@HRCDs was evaluated through in vitro cellular experiments. The B@HRCDs were finally employed for Fe3+ sensing, anti-counterfeiting, and photocatalysis applications.

The herbal medicines used in this study (Xuefu Zhuyu decoction as the example) are comprised of Semen prunus (Taoren, 12 g), Flos carthami (Honghua, 9 g), Radix Angelicae sinensis (Danggui, 9 g), Radix rehmanniae (Dihuang, 9 g), Radix Achyranthis bidentatae (Niuxi, 9 g), Rhizoma chuanxiong (Chuanxiong, 4.5 g), Radix platycodonis (Jiegeng, 4.5 g), Radix Paeoniae rubra (Chishao, 6 g), Fructus aurantii (Zhiqiao, 6 g), Rhizoma glycyrrhizae (Gancao, 6 g), and Radix Bupleuri (Chaihu, 3 g). All of them were purchased from Xiangya Hospital Pharmacy (Hunan, China). The herbal residues were obtained by boiling in deionized water, discarding the solution, and drying in an oven. Ethanol was purchased from Heng xing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Rhodamine B, indigo carmine, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), Ethylenediamine (EDA) and boric acid were obtained from Macklin Company (Shanghai, China). Zinc acetate (Zn(CH3COO)2), silver acetate (CH3COOAg), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), lead chloride (PbCl2), manganese (II) chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O), copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), nickel (II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O), iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), iron (II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), aluminum sulfate octadecahydrate (Al2(SO4)3·18H2O), and cadmium chloride hemi (pentahydrate) (CdCl2·2.5H2O) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (China) without further treatment.

The synthesis steps closely resemble the previously reported process (Liu et al., 2019), with minor adjustments. Specifically, dried herbal residues (43 g) were pulverized into a powder, soaked in ethanol, and extracted by 76 °C ethanol (150 mL) for 2 h. After collecting the ethanol extract, boric acid (4.3 g) was immediately added to the solution, and then transferred to a 250 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) autoclave. Then, the mixed solution was heated to 200 °C for 8 h. After the reaction was completed, a brown solution was obtained after the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature. The excess ethanol was removed under reduced pressure and concentrated to 20 mL. Afterward, deionized water (50–70 mL) was added to the condensed solution and then transferred into a dialysis bag with a 1000 Da molecular weight cut-off. The dialysis bag was immersed in a glass beaker filled with 2,000 mL of deionized water and then placed on a magnetic stirrer (Shanghai Sile Instruments Co, Ltd) at a rotational speed of 380 rpm at 25 °C. Changed the water every 6 h until the water around the dialysis bag became non-fluorescent. Finally, the solution was collected, and powdery brown B@HRCDs were obtained by freeze-drying for 30 h.

The morphology of the B@HRCDs was evaluated by TEM (Tecnai G2 F20, USA) at 200 kV. FTIR spectra were acquired using a Spectrum Two FTIR spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA) equipped with an ATR Diamond accessory. The spectra were recorded in the 600–4,000 cm−1 region with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and an average of 16 scans. XPS experiments were performed on a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi instrument with Al Kα X-ray radiation. Fluorescence spectroscopy was recorded on an F7000 fluorometer, with excitation and emission slit widths set at 10.0 nm. The PMT voltage was adjusted to 700 V, and scans were collected at a rate of 1,200 nm/min (Hitachi, Japan). Zeta potential was measured using a Nano ZS (Malvern, UK) at 25 °C. UV–vis spectra were collected by a GENESYS™ 180 UV–vis Spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA) using quartz cuvettes of 10 mm. XRD patterns were performed on a Miniflex 600 XRD smartlab (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (0.15406 nm). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed at 25 °C using an FM-4P-TCSPC spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, USA) with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The optimized conformation of the CDs was calculated with density functional theory (DFT) calculations by using B3LYP/6-31G(d). Frontier molecular orbital calculations were based on the 50 lowest energy singlet excited states. The conformation optimization and energy calculations were performed on a Gaussian 16 program (Frisch et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of free Fe3+ in solution
ce:section.ce:para: Iron ions were detected by fluorescence spectra in aqueous solutions. Briefly, B@HRCDs were dissolved in deionized water at a concentration of 20 μg/mL, followed by the addition of FeCl3·6H2O at different concentrations. After incubation at room temperature for 10 min, the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 445 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The selectivity of iron was measured by adding other metal ions (Ag+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Al3+, and Zn2+) at a concentration of 200 μM using the same methods.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of B@HRCDs was calculated according to the literature using quinine sulfate as the standard (0.1 M H2SO4 as a solvent, Ф
st = 0.54 at 360 nm). A corresponding QY formula is shown in equation (1) (Zhao et al., 2021).

In equation (1), Ф is the fluorescence quantum yield, I is the integral area of the emission intensity in the fluorescence spectrum, A is the absorbance intensity, and η is the refractive index of the sample. The subscript ''st'' represents the reference (quinine sulfate). To minimize the reabsorption effects, the intensity of absorbance had to be kept below 0.1 for all the measured samples.

To assess the cytotoxicity of B@HRCDs, the viability of BV2 cells was determined using the CCK8 assay. Typically, BV2 cells (1 × 104) were seeded in 96-well plates with 100 μL of culture medium. After incubation, the cells were incubated for 24 h using different concentrations of B@HRCDs added to the culture medium. Then, they were incubated with 10% Cell Counting Kit-8 solution (CCK-8) (Vazyme) for 30 min at 37 °C, and the optical density (OD) was measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader.

HeLa cells were seeded at a density of 5 × 104 (24 wells) and incubated for 24 h. B@HRCD-containing medium at a concentration of 400 μg/mL was added to HeLa cells to replace the original medium. After incubating for 12 h, the medium was discarded and the plates were washed three times with fresh phosphate buffered saline (PBS). Media containing Fe3+ at a concentration of 400 μM was added and incubated for 6 h. Fluorescence imaging was then performed using a Leica fluorescence microscope (DMi8, Leica Microsystems, Germany) after three washes with fresh PBS (blue excitation light sources).

The photocatalytic experiments were performed under sunlight irradiation. Rhodamine B and indigo carmine were selected as the target organic dyes for the photocatalytic experiments. Typically, B@HRCDs were first mixed with indigo carmine in the dark overnight to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium at room temperature. In the light reaction, the mixed solution was removed at intervals to evaluate the degradation efficiency by measuring the absorption of indigo carmine with a spectrophotometer. Quartz cuvettes with optical lengths of 1 mm and 10 mm were used to measure the UV absorption. To investigate the mechanisms of photocatalysis, active species trapping experiments were performed to evaluate reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the photocatalytic process. In this study, BQ and IPA were used as trapping agents for superoxide radicals (·O2−) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH), respectively.

Bond paper was used to conduct anti-counterfeiting experiments. The fluorescent ink was prepared from B@HRCDs in ultrapure water at 3 mg/mL. Paper cutting was soaked in fluorescent ink for 2 h and dried, and then fluorescence was observed under 385 nm excitation light. The letters and patterns were observed under UV light at a wavelength of 254 nm.

B@HRCDs were successfully synthesized using the solvothermal method, and boron was doped into the carbon skeleton. These B@HRCDs exhibit high selectivity for Fe3+, primarily due to abundant hydroxyl groups on their surface. Owing to their superior fluorescence stability, B@HRCDs are employed for anti-counterfeiting purposes. In addition, B@HRCDs exhibit excellent photocatalytic activity. A further discussion of these specific findings will be presented.

The B@HRCDs doped with boron were synthesized by a solvothermal method at a temperature of 200 °C for 8 h (Scheme 1 ). Boric acid was used as a doping reagent. In this work, the reaction conditions were optimized to obtain higher quantum yields by changing the mass ratios of boric acid and herbal residues. As shown in Table S1, the herbal residues and boric acid with a mass ratio of 10:1 (43 g/4.3 g) exhibited the highest quantum yield of 4.0% in the B-doped CDs system. Nitrogen-doped (N-doped) CDs, using EDA as the nitrogen source for doping, were also fabricated by the solvothermal method at 200 °C for 8 h (Fig. S1). In particular, the CDs obtained through pyrolysis using a muffle furnace at 350 °C for 4 h exhibited the highest quantum yield (Li et al., 2022). However, CDs doped with nitrogen as well as synthesized by pyrolysis failed to show selectivity for metal ions and could not be used for ion sensing (Fig. S2). B-doped CDs showed higher sensitivity to Fe3+ than nondoped and N-doped CDs in the same concentration of Fe3+ solution (Fig. S3 and Fig. 4c). In the blue light region, fluorescence emission faces interference limitations from the autofluorescence of biological tissues (Liu et al., 2018). Long wavelength emission CDs attracts more attention because of their ability to penetrate deeply into tissues, high resistance to photobleaching, and avoidance of interference from the fluorescence of biological samples (Lee et al., 2016). It has been reported that heteroatom doping can greatly affect optical behaviors such as emission redshift (Das et al., 2015). Through theoretical calculations, it was demonstrated that the doping of the heteroatom decreased the bandgap of the electronic transition (Fig. S4), indicating that doping allowed the emission to redshift. Unfortunately, the emission of N-doped CDs showed no significant redshift compared with nondoped CDs, while B@HRCDs were redshifted from 420 nm to 445 nm (Fig. S3 and Fig. 4). This may be caused by the unsuccessful doping of N atoms, or their possible introduction only to the surface of the CDs (Zhu et al., 2019). Finally, owing to the favorable selectivity of the B-doped system for Fe3+ by solvothermal synthesis, B@HRCDs were selected as the target of this study.

The morphology of B@HRCDs was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the particles were uniformly dispersed in water with an average diameter of approximately 3 nm (Fig. S5). In the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image (inset in Fig. 1a), clear lattice fringes with a d spacing of 0.22 nm were observed, which could be assigned to the plane (100) of graphitic carbon (Khan et al., 2018). The particle size distribution was obtained by randomly counting 50 particles from the TEM images using ImageJ software (Fig. 1b), and the average particle size was determined to be 2.8 ± 0.5 nm. A broad diffraction peak of 2θ at around 22.3° was indicated by the XRD pattern, which can be attributed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1c) (Bano et al., 2018). The charge surface of B@HRCDs was detected by using a Malvern Instrument Nano ZS. As shown in Fig. S6, the zeta potential of B@HRCDs was −12.4 mV in an aqueous solution, which clearly demonstrates that the surface of the B@HRCDs is functionalized by negatively charged groups (e.g., carboxylic acids) (Vieira et al., 2021). Surface functional groups were investigated using FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1d). As depicted in Fig. 1d, a broad and strong peak was observed in the range of 3,200–3,400 cm−1, which was assigned to the O–H and N–H stretching peaks (Huang et al., 2019). The characteristic peaks at 1,706, 1,633, 1,450, 1,364, 1,100, and 1,040 cm−1 were ascribed to OC–OH, OC–NH, CC or B–O, B–N, B–C, and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively (Wang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2009; Mannan et al., 2018).

XPS experiments was used to characterize the chemical composition of B@HRCDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, two major peaks at 284.80 eV and 532.72 eV with two small peaks at 400.09 eV and 192.04 eV were clearly observed, which were ascribed to C, O, N, and B, respectively. The results showed that B@HRCDs were mainly composed of C1s and O1s elements, with relative proportions of 74.74% and 22.22%. In addition, the elements of N1s (1.64%) and B1s (1.41%) are present at relatively low levels on the B@HRCDs surface. In the high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2b), the XPS spectrum can be divided into three peaks with binding energies at 284.67, 285.96, and 288.78 eV, corresponding to C–C, C–N, and OC–O/COOH groups (Yang et al., 2021). In Fig. 2c, the N1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 400.04 and 401.69 eV, indicating the presence of pyrroline N and graphite N (Ding et al., 2017). The proportion of pyrrole-like moieties is significantly higher than that of graphitic nitrogen, indicating that the nitrogen atoms are primarily concentrated along the edges of the B@HRCDs (Zhao et al., 2021). Moreover, the peak of B1s can be fitted with two peaks at 190.84 and 192.17 eV (Fig. 2d), which were assigned to B–N and B–CO2 groups (Wang et al., 2018). The XPS spectra align with the FT-IR patterns, indicating the presence of abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of B@HRCDs, while also confirming the successful incorporation of B atoms into the carbon skeleton.

The optical properties of the B@HRCDs in an aqueous solution were analyzed by using photoluminescence and UV–vis absorption spectra and are illustrated in Fig. 3 . As depicted in Fig. 3a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the aqueous B@HRCDs solution revealed a weak peak at approximately 280 nm, corresponding to the π-π* electronic transition of the CC bond on the carbon skeleton (Ding et al., 2017). In addition, the emission spectra of the B@HRCDs were detected with a maximum emission around 445 nm following excitation at 360 nm (Fig. 3a). The B@HRCDs exhibited wavelength-dependent fluorescence, with a gradual decrease in emission intensity accompanied by a shift in the maximum emission wavelength from 440 nm to 500 nm when the excitation wavelength increased from 360 nm to 430 nm (Fig. 3b). Digital photographs of B@HRCDs under various excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. S7. As reported in the literature, the wavelength-dependent behavior is related to the particle size as well as the degree of surface oxidation (Zhu et al., 2019). Since the B@HRCDs have a narrow size distribution, as shown in Fig. 1b, the phenomenon is ascribed to multiple fluorophores in the surface state of B@HRCDs.

The fluorescence stability of CDs in high concentrations of ions along with acids and bases is an important indicator for assessing their application in real environments, including bioimaging and sensing under different physiological conditions. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity was unchanged in the presence of NaCl at concentrations ranging from 0.2 to 1 M, indicating that the B@HRCDs can be applied to conditions with high ionic strength. Finally, to evaluate the stability of the fluorescence intensity at various pH values, the fluorescence intensity is presented in the supporting information (Fig. S9). The synthesized CDs were stable between pH 3.0 and 11.0. Therefore, the B@HRCDs exhibit remarkable fluorescence stability at high ion concentrations and in a broad pH range, which is essential for their applications in vivo imaging or sensing in living organisms and the natural environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of B@HRCDs to Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: While CDs are often reported to be used for sensing Fe3+, the lack of specificity has limited their application. According to prior research, copper and zinc ions typically interfere with the fluorescence intensity when CDs respond to Fe3+ (Hu et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2019). Next, the selectivity of B@HRCDs for Fe3+ was determined by adding other metal ions including Ag+, Al3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, and Cd2+. Fluorescence emission intensity ratios of F/F0 (F and F0 represent the fluorescence emission intensity in the presence and absence of metal ions, respectively) of various metal ions were evaluated at a concentration of 200 μM (Fig. S10). As illustrated in Fig. 4 c, the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply after the addition of Fe3+, while other metal ions had a weak influence. Digital photographs of the B@HRCDs under ambient light and UV light are shown in Fig. S11. It is noteworthy that the fluorescence quenching of Fe3+ appears to be much higher than that of Fe2+, suggesting that the high charge density leads to a higher affinity for the trivalent metal ions. However, the coordination ability of Al3+ is weaker compared to that of Fe3+, resulting in CDs exhibiting a higher selectivity for Fe3+. This selectivity towards Fe3+ arises from the ability of CDs to donate electron pairs to the excited state vacant d orbitals of Fe3+ through a non-radiative electron transfer process, facilitating the formation of surface complexes (Pandey et al., 2020). It may be hypothesized that B@HRCDs derived from biomass possess abundant functional groups, such as hydroxyl groups, are highly bound to Fe3+.

To further investigate the quenching effect of Fe3+, the concentration dependence of fluorescence spectra was measured. The fluorescence intensity of the B@HRCDs solution at 445 nm gradually decreased with increasing Fe3+ concentration accompanied by a redshift of the emission maximum wavelength (Fig. 4a). In addition, a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratios of F/F0 and Fe3+ concentration in the linear range of 0–80 μM with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.999 is shown in Fig. 4b. The limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 1.08 μM based on an S/N ratio of 3, which was lower than the CDs obtained from other biomass as precursors (Aslandas et al., 2015). Therefore, B@HRCDs have the potential to be used as sensors for the detection of Fe3+ ions. The optical properties of the CDs from waste herbal residues sources for Fe3+ detection was compared with other recently reported work. The data are listed in Table S2.

In addition, to further assess the coordination interactions between B@HRCDs and Fe3+ ions, EDTA solution was added to the B@HRCDs-Fe3+ complex to observe the restoration of fluorescence intensity. As illustrated in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence intensity of CD was markedly decreased in the presence of Fe3+ at a concentration of 200 μM. However, the fluorescence intensity of the B@HRCDs-Fe3+ complex was increased by more than 2-fold after the addition of EDTA solution (Fig. S12). The reversible fluorescence recovery suggested that the complexation of Fe3+ by EDTA and B@HRCDs was competitive. Moreover, the intensity of fluorescence changed because of the interactions of EDTA and B@HRCDs, which was much lower than that of EDTA and B@HRCDs-Fe3+ complexes. The results show that CDs are weaker than the chelating agent EDTA in complexation with Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching mechanism to detect Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching mechanism of Fe3+ was detected by recording the fluorescence lifetime in the presence and absence of Fe3+ with a transient fluorescence spectrometer. (Fig. S13). In the absence of Fe3+, the fluorescence lifetime of B@HRCDs was determined to be 0.51 ns after fitting with a third exponential equation. However, when Fe3+ was added to the mixture at concentrations of 40, 80, and 160 μM, a slight increase in lifetime was observed, corresponding to values of 0.57, 0.61, and 0.63 ns. Owing to the change in fluorescence lifetime well below an order of magnitude, static quenching occurred due to the interaction between Fe3+ ions and functional groups on the CD surface (e.g., hydroxyl groups) to generate nonfluorescent complexes (Wang et al., 2021). Absorption spectra further confirmed the static quenching mechanism. The absorption peaks of the B@HRCDs and B@HRCDs/Fe3+ systems were observed at 280 nm (Fig. S14). In comparison to the spectrum of B@HRCDs, the spectrum of the B@HRCDs/Fe3+ system exhibits a significant increase in absorption. However, it shows a slight decrease in absorption compared to the combined absorption of B@HRCDs and Fe3+. These findings confirm the formation of a ground state complex between B@HRCDs and Fe3+, indicating static quenching as the quenching mechanism of the B@HRCDs/Fe3+ system (Tan et al., 2022). The proposed mechanism of fluorescence quenching of B@HRCDs in the presence of Fe3+ is shown in Fig. 5 . These results were consistent with the fluorescence spectra of B@HRCDs-Fe3+ complexes as shown in Fig. 4a, where a small redshift of the maximum emission wavelength was found with increasing Fe3+ concentration (Zhao et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and intracellular Fe3+ detection of B@HRCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To verify that B@HRCDs can be applied as a sensor in a physiological environment, cytotoxicity and intracellular Fe3+ bioimaging were examined. First, the in vitro cytotoxicity was assessed using BV2 cells with different concentrations of B@HRCDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 a, there appeared to be no change in cell viability when exposed to B@HRCDs in the concentration range of 0–200 μg/mL, indicating that CD has good biocompatibility and very low cytotoxicity. Next, as shown in Fig. 6c, HeLa cells treated with B@HRCDs emitted bright green fluorescence upon excitation with a 480 nm light source. However, as expected, the fluorescence intensity sharply decreased after incubation with the exogenous Fe3+ solution (400 μg/mL) (Fig. 6e). These results were consistent with those observed in the spectroscopic experiments, suggesting that the prepared B@HRCDs can be used as an effective probe for intracellular Fe3+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation of indigo carmine by B@HRCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of B@HRCDs was evaluated through the degradation of organic dyes under sunlight. The dyes rhodamine B and indigo carmine were chosen for study because their UV absorption is above 550 nm and can be clearly distinguished from that of the B@HRCDs. In Fig. S15, the degradation rate of indigo carmine was higher than that of rhodamine B in solutions with B@HRCDs and dye concentrations of 100 and 5 μg/mL, respectively. To improve the photocatalytic ability, the concentration of B@HRCDs (195 μg/mL) was increased, and the photodegradation rate increased from 51% to 81% (Fig. S15c). The concentration of indigo carmine increased to 15 μg/mL because of the lower UV absorption at a concentration of 5 μg/mL. The UV absorption of indigo carmine at different times under sunlight irradiation was investigated to give a more intuitive understanding of the photodegradation kinetics. As shown in Fig. S15d, the degradation rate of indigo carmine reached 85% after 4 h of sunlight irradiation (10 mm path-length cuvette). To increase the degradation efficiency of indigo carmine, the exposure time to sunlight was further increased to 5 h. The degradation efficiency of indigo carmine (45 μg/mL) was determined by UV spectroscopy using a 1 mm path length cuvette and reached 91% after 5 h of irradiation (Fig. 7 c). Photographs of indigo carmine before and after degradation by sunlight are shown in Fig. 7a. A photograph of bottle #3 without B@HRCDs was exposed to sunlight for 5 h, while bottles #4, #5, #6, #7, and #8 with B@HRCDs were exposed to sunlight for 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 h, respectively. In Fig. 7f, the stability and recycling experiments of B@HRCDs were also investigated. The percentage of photodegradation of indigo carmine changed from 91% to 61% after 5 recycling cycles, indicating the excellent stability and recyclability of B@HRCDs. To study the photocatalytic mechanism, active species trapping experiments were performed (Fig. 7e). Diverse scavengers were used in the reaction, including IPA for scavenging the free radical ·OH and BQ for ·O2−. In this study, the photodegradation efficiencies decreased from 91% to 83% and 75% at 5 h after adding BQ and IPA, indicating that ·OH acted as the main ROS in the photodegradation process. Potential mechanisms for the generation of ·OH and the pathway of indigo carmine degradation are shown in Fig. 7d and b.

In addition, B@HRCDs were applied to anti-counterfeiting. To avoid fluorescent interference, bond paper was used for handwriting, painting and hand-cutting. In Fig. 8 a, the letters “CARBON DOTS” handwritten with B@HRCDs ink were nearly invisible under daylight, while the letters emitted blue fluorescence when exposed to a 254 nm UV lamp. Similarly, the patterns of dogs, elephants, and dolphins displayed clear blue fluorescence under 254 nm irradiation (Fig. 8b). However, when the UV lamp was turned off, these patterns were not easily recognized by the naked eye under daylight. The handmade paper cuttings showed bright fluorescence under 385 nm excitation after 2 h of soaking in the B@HRCDs solution (Fig. 8c). Conversely, the paper cuttings without soaked B@HRCDs failed to show fluorescence emission under 385 nm excitation light. Viewed together, these features can be used for information encryption involving a high level of security, such as special logos on banknotes.

In summary, B@HRCDs derived from herbal medicine were synthesized with green solvothermal methods. Interestingly, the as-prepared B@HRCDs exhibited low cytotoxicity and excellent Fe3+ selectivity and can be used as sensors to detect the presence of Fe3+ in HeLa cells. Compared with undoped CDs, the doping of B atoms increases the affinity of CDs with Fe3+, resulting in a higher degree of fluorescence quenching at the same concentration of Fe3+. In addition, the B@HRCDs exhibit bright solid fluorescence on bond paper, which can be used for message encryption. With sunlight irradiation, B@HRCDs displayed favorable photocatalytic activity. The degradation rate of indigo carmine was 91% after 5 h of sunlight irradiation, and the rate decreased to 61% after 5 cycles, indicating that B@HRCDs have good photostability and photocatalytic activity. This work presents a practical approach to producing cleaner functional products by utilizing bulk waste from herbal residues, showcasing the significant potential to enhance resource utilization of herbs and reduce environmental waste.
